items.q: What does the 'tragedy of the commons' describe?
items.correct: Shared resources tend to be overexploited because individuals benefit personally while sharing costs.
items.misconception: It is a play about poor people.
items.why: Students take 'commons' as a place. Hardin's metaphor describes a STRUCTURAL PROBLEM: rational individual choices lead to collective ruin when costs are externalised - applies to overfishing, climate, and groundwater.

items.q: Why does renewable energy require energy storage solutions?
items.correct: Solar and wind generation are intermittent - storage smooths out supply and demand.
items.misconception: Renewable energy can never meet demand.
items.random_wrong: Because they generate too much energy.
items.why: Students dismiss renewables as inadequate. The challenge is intermittency, not insufficiency - the Sun sets, wind drops. Storage (batteries, pumped hydro) addresses timing, not total capacity.

items.q: How are most microplastics formed in the environment?
items.correct: Larger plastic items break down physically into smaller fragments over time.
items.misconception: They are all manufactured at microscopic size deliberately.
items.why: Students think microplastics are designed small. Most are SECONDARY - formed when sun, waves, and wear break down larger items. Primary microplastics (microbeads, fibres) are a smaller proportion.

items.q: Who argued food can't keep up with population (1798)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Malthus. “Darwin” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Shift from high to low birth/death rates is called?
items.why: It's Demographic transition. “Carrying capacity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 8 billion. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
